topic: BOU 2017 eclipse -10.9 nT — change-driven c5 (FSF empirical-fitting claim is quantitatively wrong)
change_summary: detail_verdict_text grew from 2469 -> 3267 chars (+32%, +798 chars) since c4 (2026-04-14). New content: (a) Meza et al. (2021) citation expanded with full journal name + DOI 10.1029/2020JA028746 (commit bc600a8, 2026-04-14, ISS-991 self-apply); (b) Kp language softened from 'disturbed' to 'unsettled' with explicit G-scale reference; (c) explicit post-hoc paragraph about repository history and timestamping; (d) added 'The dome's refraction cascade (see Section 1.8)...' linking sentence; (e) added triple-count note naming WIN-010/WIN-025/WIN-068. H(r) typo (exp(-1r/...)) fixed. FSF 32° of latitude framing adopted.
our_argument_summary: Std Model Explains: Chapman (1933) ionospheric Sq mechanism (cited with Meza 2021 + DOI) predicts the BOU 2017 -10.9 nT signal; the dome reuses that observation as both a WIN and its 2026 FSF baseline (training-as-test); the FSF table is claimed empirical, not derivable from H(r); Kp asymmetric standard (3-4 accepted as baseline, <2 required for 2026 test); post-hoc repository ordering; triple-count across WIN-010/025/068.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-010 reports BOU 2017 Aug 21 eclipse, -10.9 nT at 17:20 UTC. The 2026 extension uses delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF with baseline = BOU 2017 (-10.9 nT) or W004 empirical (-22.24 nT) and per-station FSF 0.642-2.075 (raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 26-34). The FSF values are stated to derive from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' (line 22).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: BOU 2017 value is a hardcoded historical anchor. 2026 station predictions combine (a) standard Besselian eclipse coverage, (b) hardcoded BOU/W004 baseline, (c) an FSF table now empirically reverse-derivable from H(r)/r (see quantitative_verification below).
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp < 2 quiet-day precondition for 2026 test voids the validation on any geomagnetically unsettled day.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: CRITICAL: The review claims the FSF table is 'consistent with empirical fitting, not dome-geometric derivation'. Direct computation shows the opposite. With BOU as the normalization station (40.015 degrees N in the dome's V12 scheme, solving r * tan(theta) = H(r) gives r_BOU = 5421 km, H(r_BOU)/r_BOU = 0.840), the formula FSF = (H(r_station)/r_station) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) reproduces all nine published FSF values to better than 0.2 percent for seven stations and within 1.8 percent for the remaining two (SPT, EBR). Pearson r between FSF and H/r is 0.9999. The dome's claim 'FSF = field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' is numerically correct. Our review's central quantitative rebuttal of FSF is wrong.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: 2017 WIN-010 value is a static historical observation; 2026 monitor extension uses hardcoded BOU/W004 baselines multiplied by a hardcoded FSF table. No API call produces the underlying -10.9 nT number. 'hardcoded' remains correct.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933) + modern Sq modeling (Kim & Chang 2018; numerous TIEGCM/WACCM-X studies) reproduce eclipse magnetic perturbations without dome parameters. The dome adopts the signal and relabels the mechanism as 'firmament cavity induction'. Tag unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: BOU 2017 magnetogram is in the public INTERMAGNET archive from 2017 onward, predating v51 by 9 years; the dome lists it as a Baseline input for 2026, so the confirming observation is also a parameter. Tag unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Qualified false. The FSF scaling IS geometrically derived from H(r)/r as claimed (quantitative verification confirms to 0.1 percent at most stations). However, (a) the absolute magnitude at BOU itself is adopted from observation, not derived, and (b) the H(r)/r scaling is a station-to-station ratio, not a first-principles magnitude prediction. The tag remains false because the WIN-010 -10.9 nT number is not derived from dome parameters, but the evidence text needs rewriting: we cannot claim 'FSF is empirical' — it is geometrically derived, just not sufficient to predict the anchor.

holes_found.description: FACTUAL ERROR in detail_verdict_text: 'FSF values range from 0.642 to 2.075 across nine stations — a 3.2x spread across ~32 degrees of European latitude [...] The FSF values are empirically fitted to observed eclipse data, not derived from dome parameters.' Direct numerical check refutes this. With BOU as the normalization station (lat 40.015, r_BOU ~5421 km via the dome's own V12 formula, H(r_BOU)/r_BOU = 0.8395), the formula FSF = (H(r_station)/r_station) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) reproduces the dome's published FSF values to within 0.1 percent for seven of nine stations (ESK, LER, HAD, NGK, SNK, CLF, COI) and within 0.5-1.8 percent for EBR and SPT. Pearson r between computed H/r and claimed FSF = 0.9999. The dome's statement 'FSF = field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' is numerically correct. Our review's central quantitative rebuttal of the FSF derivation is falsified by a five-line Python script any dome defender can run. This is the exact pattern of error the review exists to catch, and it now lives in our own text.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the FSF paragraph in detail_verdict_text. The correct argument is: (1) even granting FSF = (H/r at station) / (H/r at BOU) as a valid geometric scaling — which it is, to 0.1 percent — the scaling only predicts ratios between stations, not the absolute magnitude at BOU; (2) the BOU magnitude itself (-10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation, not derived from dome parameters, so the 'geometric derivation' of the other stations is anchored to a measured baseline; (3) standard ionospheric Sq modeling predicts absolute magnitudes at each station from electron density and Sq-current geometry, which is stronger than the dome's ratio-scaling + observed anchor. The debunk still holds (training-as-test + Chapman non-discrimination), but must not assert 'empirical fitting' — the dome's FSF really is H/r.

holes_found.description: UNVERIFIED CITATION NOW DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS WIN-068. detail_verdict_text cites 'Meza et al. (2021, Journal of Geophysical Research: Space Physics, doi:10.1029/2020JA028746)' as the quantitative ionospheric Sq source. ISS-991 was closed on 2026-04-14 by commit bc600a8 by 'expanding the citation with full journal name + DOI' — but 'adding a DOI' is not 'verifying the DOI resolves to a real paper that says what we claim'. Two days later, commit d6a5524 (2026-04-16) explicitly REMOVED the exact same Meza et al. (2021) citation from WIN-068's detail_verdict_text with rationale 'remove unverifiable Meza et al. 2021 citation (2 cycles unresolved)'. So the project now simultaneously asserts Meza 2021 as authoritative (WIN-010, with DOI) and acknowledges it as unverifiable (WIN-068, removed). A reader who cross-reads the two panels — or an adversarial AI that greps for 'Meza' across the site — will flag this in seconds. If the DOI is wrong, we have another WIN-008/009/014 citation error on public record.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) independently verify 10.1029/2020JA028746 resolves to a specific Meza et al. paper on eclipse ionosphere with the cited content, documenting the verification in citation_check; OR (b) remove the Meza citation and replace with Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054), which is already verified and used in WIN-068's detail_evidence. Option (b) is preferred — it aligns the three-WIN eclipse cluster on a single verified anchor, closes the cross-WIN inconsistency, and removes DOI-wrong-answer tail risk. The 'quantitatively modeled by' phrasing in WIN-010 can survive the swap with minimal rewording.

holes_found.description: STALE CROSS-REFERENCE: detail_verdict_text says 'The dome's refraction cascade (see Section 1.8) further undermines the FSF's geometric derivation claim — if the dome's own n(r) medium makes the sun's physical position seasonally indeterminate, the FSF ratio anchored to BOU dome-coordinate geometry inherits that indeterminacy.' Two problems: (1) Section 1.8 was rewritten per HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001 on 2026-04-12 from 'The Refraction Cascade' to 'The Coordinate Collapse: One Fix Destroys the Map'. A reader clicking through from WIN-010 expects 'cascade' content and finds coordinate-collapse content. (2) The one-sentence summary ('sun's physical position seasonally indeterminate') describes the OLD Section 1.8 framing. The CURRENT Section 1.8 argues about Polaris-angle distortion contaminating the coordinate system, not seasonal sun indeterminacy. Both the section name and the summary are out of date. Independently: the sentence's 'FSF ratio anchored to BOU dome-coordinate geometry inherits that indeterminacy' is also inconsistent with the corrected FSF finding above — if FSF really is H(r)/r geometry, we need a different reason why Section 1.8's contamination propagates (answer: r values computed from contaminated Polaris angles, so H(r)/r itself is wrong).
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the entire sentence with one that (a) uses the current Section 1.8 framing and (b) survives the corrected FSF finding. Suggested: 'Even if FSF = H(r)/r as the dome claims, Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse) shows the r values themselves are computed from Polaris elevation angles contaminated by the same n(r) medium; every dome-coordinate r in the FSF table inherits the contamination, so the claimed geometric derivation anchors on numbers that are not what the dome thinks they are.' This threads the needle: it no longer claims 'FSF is empirical', uses the current Section 1.8 title, and repurposes the coordinate-contamination argument to reach the same destination (the FSF derivation is compromised for a reason that survives computation).

holes_found.description: ADVOCATE MODE PRONG A (Chapman is qualitative) STILL UNPATCHED FROM C4. The c4 review recommended citing Kim & Chang (2018) or a quantitative modern Sq model alongside Chapman (1933) to defeat the defender argument 'Chapman is qualitative; you are smuggling in modern ionospheric models that have free parameters'. The c5 text still leans only on Meza (2021) — which, per the MAJOR finding above, is itself unverified/contradicted by WIN-068. After swapping to Kim & Chang, this hole closes automatically, so the fix is one-and-done with the citation swap.
holes_found.recommendation: Fix together with the Meza citation swap. Kim & Chang (2018) is real, peer-reviewed, and verified; citing it for the quantitative Sq match closes both Prong A and the Meza inconsistency in a single edit.

holes_found.description: ADVOCATE MODE PRONG B (similar disc radii) UNACHIEVABLE — the argument itself is dead on arrival. The c4 review recommended computing specific station pairs with near-identical dome-coordinate r but sharply different FSF, to defeat the defender rebuttal 'stations aren't at similar r in dome coordinates'. When I compute the r values via r * tan(latitude) = H(r): SNK sits at r=7021 km, LER at r=3330 km, with intermediate stations in between. FSF correlates with H(r)/r at r=0.9999. There is no station pair with 'similar r but different FSF' in this dataset. The defender wins the factual dispute here. The correct conclusion is that the review should not assert 'stations at similar disc radii' — they are not, and their FSF values track the very geometry the dome claims.
holes_found.recommendation: Delete the phrase 'among stations at similar disc radii' wherever it appears in detail_verdict_text (and detail_evidence if present). The argument should not rest on a similar-r claim at all. Pivot to the ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude argument from the CRITICAL hole above.

holes_found.description: ADVOCATE MODE PRONG C (empirical tuning) UNADDRESSED. The c4 recommendation was to distinguish the dome's in-sample reuse of BOU 2017 from standard ionospheric models' calibration-against-historical-data + prediction-on-future-eclipses pattern. The c5 text has the 'training data cannot serve as test data' line (good) but does not preempt the symmetric defender argument 'Sq models also tune parameters against historical eclipses; you are applying a double standard.' Combined with the now-falsified 'empirical fitting' framing, this prong is the larger structural risk.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence to the FSF rewrite: 'Standard Sq models calibrate conductivity and current-system parameters once against the literature, then predict future, independent events; the dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both a counted WIN and the 2026 baseline, which is in-sample evaluation, not out-of-sample calibration.' Ties the rebuttal back to training-as-test, which is the clean part of the argument.

holes_found.description: KP VALUE STILL NOT PRECISELY SOURCED. The c5 text says '2017 baseline underlying this WIN was recorded at Kp = 3-4 (unsettled — NOAA G-scale geomagnetic disturbance begins at Kp 5 / G1)'. C4 recommended either (a) quoting the exact 3-hour NOAA/GFZ Kp index for the 17:20 UTC interval with a source link, or (b) softening with a cite. The soften has been partly done (good: 'unsettled' replacing 'disturbed'; good: explicit G-scale threshold). But the '3-4' range is still asserted without a citation, and three-hour Kp indices give a single value per 3-hour window, not a range — '3 to 4' describes neighboring intervals or uncertainty in the exact Kp reading. A defender can still say 'which is it, 3 or 4, and where is your source?'
holes_found.recommendation: Either pin to a single value with a link to the NOAA SWPC or GFZ Potsdam Kp archive for 2017-08-21 15:00-18:00 UT, or change '3-4' to 'approximately 3' (the quieter neighbor of the two, conservative for our argument). Either change removes the debating handle without weakening the asymmetric-standard argument.

holes_found.description: STALE TERMINOLOGY: the word 'refraction cascade' appears in detail_verdict_text. Per HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001, Section 1.8 no longer uses 'cascade' framing — that was replaced by 'coordinate collapse'. Keeping 'refraction cascade' as the phrase WIN-010 uses to reference Section 1.8 is stale project vocabulary and compounds the bad cross-reference (major hole above). Not standalone-major, but worth fixing in the same edit pass.
holes_found.recommendation: Drop 'refraction cascade' language. Replace with 'coordinate collapse' or 'refraction fix' depending on which angle the rewritten sentence takes.

stronger_arguments.description: The ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude argument is significantly stronger than 'FSF is empirical' because (a) it concedes the geometric derivation the dome genuinely has, which removes the defender's cleanest counter; (b) it exposes what the dome has NOT derived, which is the absolute magnitude at the BOU anchor; (c) it re-ties to the training-as-test critique, which the dome cannot answer without admitting BOU 2017 is both WIN and parameter. Argumentatively: we should always pick the weakest claim the dome makes and stand there, not attack the strongest.

DOME-FSF FALSIFIABLE ON 2026-08-12: As the c4 noted, the dome publishes specific per-station FSF predictions for 2026. Since the FSF formula is now confirmed to be H(r)/r relative to BOU, the 2026 eclipse is a clean 9-point test. We should flag that the predicted delta_Z values at E001-E009 (EBR -10.7, SPT -9.8, ESK -10.3, LER -9.5, HAD -12.8, NGK -6.7, SNK -5.0, CLF -10.1, COI -10.1) are directly checkable against INTERMAGNET 2026 data post-eclipse, and we will update this WIN when that data arrives. Turns a soft Std Model Explains into a concrete upcoming test window.

CHAPMAN + KIM & CHANG COVERAGE MATCHING: The strongest way to win the Chapman-vs-dome comparison is to note that Kim & Chang (2018) report the ensemble Y-component increase at magnetic latitudes 30-50 degrees over ~180-minute windows — a pattern that tracks eclipse SHADOW GEOMETRY (Besselian-elements coverage at each station), not dome coordinates. The 2026 FSF-vs-shadow-coverage prediction is directly falsifiable: if delta_Z tracks coverage more tightly than it tracks FSF, the dome's scaling is post-hoc; if FSF beats coverage, something non-trivial is going on. This gives WIN-010 a future verdict-update path beyond 'non-discriminating forever'.
sources: Kim & Chang (2018) Adv. Space Res. 61:2040-2054 (already verified in WIN-068)

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome is genuinely correct that the 2017 BOU -10.9 nT observation exists, is recorded at eclipse maximum, matches the shadow geometry, and fits the Chapman ionospheric framework. Credited: they are right to classify the signal as EM-ionospheric rather than thermal or gravitational, and — crucially in light of the corrected FSF finding — they are right that FSF = H(r)/r is a clean geometric scaling from dome parameters. This is a more legitimate geometric derivation than our review has been claiming.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being correct about the scaling law between stations is not the same as predicting the signal. The magnitude at the anchor (BOU -10.9 nT) is adopted from observation, not derived. The scaling H(r)/r is mathematically unique to the dome only if H(r) = 8537 exp(-r/8619) is — but any smooth height-vs-radius function plus the r*tan(theta) = H(r) convention would produce a monotonic scaling of the same shape, so the match doesn't uniquely identify the dome. And standard ionospheric physics predicts absolute magnitudes directly from first principles. The WIN remains non-discriminating — the dome has a scaling law that fits, not a magnitude prediction that discriminates.

deeper_analysis:
This review reverses a key conclusion of c4. The c4 review rated our_code_claims_accurate: true and cited the FSF argument as one of the review's strongest points. Direct numerical verification — which neither c2, c3, nor c4 performed — shows the claim is quantitatively false. This is exactly the failure mode the project's own methodological principles warn against ('we criticize the dome for hardcoding numbers; we must verify our own numbers'). The review has been running for four cycles asserting that a 5-line computation does not hold. Any dome defender with Python and a pocket calculator can refute our central FSF rebuttal today.

The critical pivot is that the FSF derivation IS valid dome geometry (FSF = H(r)/r normalized to BOU, Pearson r=0.9999). The review must retreat from 'FSF is not derived' and advance to 'FSF is derived but only as a ratio, and the anchor is an observation, not a prediction'. This is a harder-to-attack position that correctly describes what the dome has done.

The Meza citation inconsistency is the second-most-urgent fix. WIN-068 removed Meza as unverifiable on 2026-04-16; WIN-010 added a DOI on 2026-04-14. The sequence looks like ISS-991 was closed by formally satisfying 'add a DOI' without doing the verification the c4 review actually asked for. Kim & Chang (2018) already exists in WIN-068 and solves the problem.

The Section 1.8 cross-reference staleness is the third. It's a simple rewrite but ties to the FSF fix: once we accept FSF = H(r)/r is geometrically correct, the bridge to Section 1.8 has to land on 'r itself is contaminated by n(r)' rather than 'sun altitude is seasonally indeterminate'.

All three are solvable in one integrated patch to detail_verdict_text. I do NOT recommend a verdict change — Std Model Explains still holds (Chapman predicts the magnitude; BOU is training-test; post-hoc ordering; triple-count). But the text that supports the verdict needs major structural editing, not a minor polish.

On the meta-point: this is the first review that exercises Lens C (Quantitative Verification) to refute a prior review's conclusion rather than confirm it. That's the lens working as intended. It should be applied to every quantitative claim in every eclipse WIN (and probably the whole kill-shot cluster) before the 2026-08-12 eclipse, because that is the most plausible date for an external adversarial read.

citations_verified: Chapman (1933) Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38, 175 — attribution widely reproduced; used consistently across WIN-010/025/068
INTERMAGNET BOU magnetograms — public archive, confirmed
Dome's raw-text/03-predictions.txt FSF table values (0.642-2.075) — verified line-by-line
citations_failed: 'FSF values [...] empirically fitted to observed eclipse data, not derived from dome parameters' — THE CLAIM ITSELF FAILS verification. FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) matches dome's published values to 0.1 percent at 7 of 9 stations, 1.8 percent at worst station. Pearson r=0.9999. The derivation the dome claims IS what the numbers show.
citations_unchecked: Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics, doi:10.1029/2020JA028746) — DOI was added on 2026-04-14 by ISS-991 fix but never verified end-to-end (author list / paper content / does DOI resolve). Simultaneously removed from WIN-068 as unverifiable on 2026-04-16. Must be verified or swapped out.

The claim that 'standard Sq models predict the 2017 BOU -10.9 nT to within measurement uncertainty' — plausible but not verified in this review; would benefit from a numerical comparison between Kim & Chang (2018) ensemble means and BOU 2017 specifically.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender running a five-line Python script can show the review's central quantitative rebuttal of FSF is wrong. The defender's case: 'The dome says FSF = V12 H(r)/r dome geometry. Take the nine FSF values in raw-text/03-predictions.txt. Compute r for each station by solving r*tan(latitude) = 8537*exp(-r/8619), the dome's own V12 formula. Compute H(r)/r. Normalize to Boulder (r_BOU ~ 5421 km). The resulting ratios match the dome's published FSF values to better than one tenth of one percent at seven of nine stations. The dome is telling the truth about its geometric derivation. The review's claim that FSF is empirically fitted, not geometrically derived, is the kind of error this review exists to catch in the dome model — and here it lives in the review itself, three cycles deep.' This is a 5/5 defense: it requires a text change in the review to neutralize. The defender does not even need to defend the dome's physics; they only need to catch us being wrong about the math the dome openly published.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Execute the CRITICAL-hole rewrite above BEFORE the dome author (or any adversarial reader) finds the Python check. Concede the FSF = H(r)/r derivation explicitly in the text, pivot to the ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude argument, and keep the training-as-test + Chapman + post-hoc + triple-count pillars intact. The Advocate now has no foothold — they would need to argue the absolute BOU magnitude is derived from dome parameters, which neither the dome itself nor any dome document claims.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Two cross-WIN inconsistencies found this cycle. (1) Meza et al. (2021) is cited in WIN-010 with a DOI as authoritative; the same citation was explicitly removed from WIN-068 on 2026-04-16 (commit d6a5524) as 'unverifiable, 2 cycles unresolved'. The project simultaneously asserts and disavows the same citation. (2) WIN-010 tells readers to see Section 1.8 for 'the refraction cascade'; Section 1.8 was renamed to 'The Coordinate Collapse' on 2026-04-12 per HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001 and no longer contains 'cascade' framing. The WIN-010 one-sentence summary ('sun's physical position seasonally indeterminate') describes the OLD Section 1.8, not the current one. Against WIN-012 (Self-Contradicted on kappa=1.67 nT/uGal): still internally consistent once the WIN-010 text is corrected, because WIN-012's numerator is WIN-010's observation which remains a Chapman-explainable value regardless of the FSF derivation question. Against WIN-013/014 (Misleading SG null results): neutral — unrelated to the FSF/Meza/Section-1.8 issues. Against WIN-025 and WIN-068 (Std Model Explains, Chapman framing): the triple-count note in WIN-010 is correct; once Meza is swapped for Kim & Chang across the cluster, the three panels will use a single verified anchor.

claims_checked: FSF values range 0.642-2.075 across nine stations (dome's raw text) — verified: values read from raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 26-34 exactly.
'3.2x spread across ~32 degrees of European latitude' — verified: 2.075/0.642 = 3.232; latitude span 28.30 (SNK) to 60.14 (LER) = 31.84 degrees. OK.

'FSF values empirically fitted to observed eclipse data, not derived from dome parameters' — FALSIFIED. Computation: for each station at latitude theta, solve r * tan(theta) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) via bisection, compute H(r)/r, divide by H(r_BOU)/r_BOU (BOU at 40.015 deg N, r_BOU = 5421.2 km, H(r_BOU)/r_BOU = 0.8395). Predicted FSF vs claimed FSF: EBR 1.034 vs 1.029 (+0.48%); SPT 0.984 vs 1.001 (-1.74%); ESK 1.7215 vs 1.722 (-0.03%); LER 2.0748 vs 2.075 (-0.01%); HAD 1.4704 vs 1.471 (-0.04%); NGK 1.5284 vs 1.529 (-0.04%); SNK 0.6414 vs 0.642 (-0.10%); CLF 1.3243 vs 1.325 (-0.06%); COI 1.0073 vs 1.008 (-0.07%). Pearson r = 0.9999. 7 of 9 stations match within 0.1 percent; worst case 1.74 percent. FSF is geometrically derived from H(r)/r as the dome claims.
'H(r) = 8537 exp(-r/8619) km' — verified formula matches detail_evidence and dome's raw text.
kappa = 10.9 nT / 6.5 uGal = 1.677 (WIN-012 cross-ref) — verified but not WIN-010's responsibility.
quantitative_verification.computations: All arithmetic run in node v18 on the fresh clone. FSF prediction script: bisection solver for r * tan(theta) = 8537*exp(-r/8619), then evaluate H(r)/r and normalize to BOU (theta = 40.015 deg N). Station latitudes sourced from IAGA station coordinates (standard references for INTERMAGNET stations). Ebro (EBR) latitude = 40.96, San Pablo (SPT) = 39.55, Eskdalemuir (ESK) = 55.32, Lerwick (LER) = 60.14, Hartland (HAD) = 50.99, Niemegk (NGK) = 52.07, Canary Islands observatory (SNK, Maspalomas) = 28.30, Chambon-la-Foret (CLF) = 48.03, Coimbra (COI) = 40.22. Boulder (BOU) = 40.015.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Our review's central quantitative claim about FSF is wrong. The dome's FSF table is reproducible from dome geometry to 0.1 percent at 7/9 stations, 1.8 percent at worst. The review must retract the 'empirical fitting' framing and pivot to the ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude argument.
